content.problemStatement:
Given a `N x N` binary matrix, find the area of maximal square submatrix in it which has all 1s.

For eg. in the following matrix, the area of maximal submatrix with all 1's is: **4** (2x2 submatrix)




Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer NNN, denoting the no. of rows and columns in the binary matrix.
Next NNN lines contains NNN space separated integers, the elements of the matrix.


Output Format

Output on a single line the area of maximal square submatrix having all 1's
constraints: Each matrix element is either 0 or 1
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains an integer N, the size of the square binary matrix. The next N lines each contain N space‑separated integers (0 or 1) representing the matrix rows.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the area of the largest square sub‑matrix that consists only of 1s.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The only test case has a 3×3 matrix. The largest all‑1 square is the 2×2 block formed by rows 2‑3 and columns 2‑3, giving area 4.
A 2×2 matrix of all 1s forms a square of side 2, area 4.
The single cell is 0, so no square of 1s exists; the maximal area is 0.

solution.approach: For each cell (i,j) we compute the side length of the largest all‑1 square whose bottom‑right corner is at (i,j). If the cell itself is 0 the value is 0. Otherwise, dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]). While filling the DP table we keep the maximum side length seen; its square is the answer. This works because a square of side k ending at (i,j) requires three neighbouring squares of side at least k-1. The algorithm runs in O(N^2) time and O(N^2) extra space per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case, O(T·N^2) overall.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2) auxiliary space for the DP table (can be reduced to O(N) but not required).
